phases.pali: sattānaṁ visuddhiyā,

tips: The root 'satta' means a living being or creature.
Literally 'one who is attached' to existence.
Pāli marks 'belonging to' on the noun itself.
The prefix 'vi-' adds intensity or distinction.
The root 'sudh' means to be clean or pure.
An abstract noun marker, turning 'pure' into 'purity'.
The -yā ending here marks the purpose: 'for the sake of'.

phases.pali: dukkhadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamāya,

tips: Specifically physical pain or the general unsatisfactoriness of life.
Pāli marks 'belonging to' on the noun itself.
attha: Home, disappearance, or setting (like the sun).
The -āya ending means 'for the purpose of'
Dative singular: indicates the goal of the practice.

phases.pali: nibbānassa sacchikiriyāya,

tips: Nibbāna: The quenching of a fire
The complete cooling of mental defilements
Marks the goal toward which the action is directed
The act of performing or realizing
Connecting sound (hiatus-bridger) between the root and the ending
The -āya ending means 'for the purpose of' (dative)
Indicates the aim of the entire practice

phases.pali: yadidaṁ cattāro satipaṭṭhānā.

tips: yad + idaṁ = "namely, that is to say"
The relative pronoun 'which' (ya) in its neuter form.
The 'd' is a sandhi consonant used to join words.
Together with 'yad', it functions like 'namely' or 'that is to say'.
The -ṁ ending marks the neuter singular case.
The -o ending is masculine plural here, matching the noun it describes.
Pāli matches the number's ending to the noun's gender and case.
The root 'smṛ' means to remember or keep in mind.
Sati: mindfulness, presence of mind, or awareness.

phases.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;

tips: vi + nī = to remove / lead away
The -ya ending marks a completed prerequisite action
loka: the world / plane of experience

Points to the specific context of practice

The -e ending is vocative plural — used when calling out to a group of monks.

The -e ending is locative — meaning 'in' or 'regarding' the body.
The long -ā is a sandhi fusion with the next segment
anu + √pass: to see along / to observe

phases.pali: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.

phases.pali: Uddeso niṭṭhito.

tips: The brief statement before detailed explanation
ud + desa = 'pointing up' or a brief summary
The -o ending marks the subject (nominative singular)
ni + ṭṭhā = to stand finished or concluded
The -ta suffix makes the verb a past participle — 'stood' or 'finished'

phases.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu araññagato vā rukkhamūlagato vā suññāgāragato vā nisīdati, pallaṅkaṁ ābhujitvā, ujuṁ kāyaṁ paṇidhāya, parimukhaṁ satiṁ upaṭṭhapetvā.

tips: The -ati ending marks the 3rd person singular present: 'he sits'
Can mean full lotus or seated posture

Accusative singular: the body as the object being set straight

phases.pali: So satova assasati, satova passasati.

tips: Sandhi consonant used to join vowels
Thematic vowel connecting root to ending

phases.pali: rassaṁ vā assasanto ‘rassaṁ assasāmī’ti pajānāti, rassaṁ vā passasanto ‘rassaṁ passasāmī’ti pajānāti.

tips: Describes the duration or depth of breath
The -ṁ ending here makes the adjective an adverb: 'shortly' or 'lightly'
Indicates an alternative possibility in the practice
The prefix 'a' indicates the inward direction
The -anto ending means 'while doing' — it describes the person currently breathing in
The prefix 'pa' indicates the outward direction of breath
The -mi ending means 'I am doing' — the practitioner's internal observation

phases.pali: ‘Sabbakāyapaṭisaṁvedī assasissāmī’ti sikkhati, ‘sabbakāyapaṭisaṁvedī passasissāmī’ti sikkhati.

tips: Paṭisaṁ: Back to / In return / Fully
The -issa marker makes the verb future — 'I will'
The -mi ending marks the first person — 'I'
√sikkh: To train / To practice / To learn
The -ati ending marks the third person — 'he/she/they practice'

phases.pali: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, dakkho bhamakāro vā bhamakārantevāsī vā dīghaṁ vā añchanto ‘dīghaṁ añchāmī’ti pajānāti, rassaṁ vā añchanto ‘rassaṁ añchāmī’ti pajānāti;

tips: The -e ending is vocative plural — used when calling out to a group.
Vedic dakṣa, Greek δεξιός, Latin decet
📝 The turner is not a passive
A long, slow pull on the lathe
Adverbial accusative: deeply / for a long time.
Adverbial: shallowly / for a short time.

phases.pali: ‘sabbakāyapaṭisaṁvedī assasissāmī’ti sikkhati, ‘sabbakāyapaṭisaṁvedī passasissāmī’ti sikkhati;

tips: The physical body as a collection
The -e ending means 'in' or 'on' the body (locative)
The long 'ā' is a joining vowel

phases.pali: Iti ajjhattaṁ vā kāye kāyānupassī viharati, bahiddhā vā kāye kāyānupassī viharati, ajjhattabahiddhā vā kāye kāyānupassī viharati;

tips: Compound joining: both internally and externally
Signals the conclusion of a section or a summary
Used here to list different perspectives of observation

The long 'ā' is a joining vowel in this compound
Observing the physical process in others

phases.pali: samudayadhammānupassī vā kāyasmiṁ viharati, vayadhammānupassī vā kāyasmiṁ viharati, samudayavayadhammānupassī vā kāyasmiṁ viharati.

tips: Full stem: dhamma (final vowel elided in sandhi before ānu)

tips: Yāva: As far as / To the extent
yāva: As far as / as much as
Not for ego, not for becoming

phases.pali: ‘Atthi kāyo’ti vā panassa sati paccupaṭṭhitā hoti. Yāvadeva ñāṇamattāya paṭissatimattāya anissito ca viharati, na ca kiñci loke upādiyati.

Refers here to the physical process

The quality of keeping the object in mind
yāva: as far as / to the extent

phases.pali: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā kurūsu viharati kammāsadhammaṁ nāma kurūnaṁ nigamo.

tips: The name of a specific town in the Kuru country.
Links proper name to common noun
Kuru: the name of the people.
"A town" — what we are talking about
ni + gama = a place where people 'go down' into (a market town).
The -ṁ ending marks the time when something happens (accusative of time).
The root 'i' means to go; 'aya' is the act of going.
sam + aya = a coming together / occasion
From the root 'bhaj' meaning to share or divide.
Kuru: the name of the people and their land.
The -su ending means 'among' or 'in' a group (locative plural).
The root 'har' means to carry or take.
vi + har = to spend time / to dwell.

phases.pali: “bhikkhavo”ti.

tips: The root 'bhikkh' relates to religious mendicancy — living on alms.
Same stem as 'bhikkhū' seen in previous phases.
The -o ending is vocative plural — used when calling out to a group.
Directly addresses the monks to get their attention.
A marker that functions like quotation marks.
Indicates the end of the spoken address.

phases.pali: Bhagavā etadavoca:

tips: The root 'bhaj' means to share or divide — here referring to one who possesses good fortune or blessings.
The -vā ending is a possessive marker meaning 'one who has'.
Nominative singular — the subject of the sentence.
Etad: This — a demonstrative pronoun pointing to the speech that follows.
The 'a-' prefix is an augment that marks the past tense in Pāli verbs.
The root 'vac' means to speak or say. The form 'voc' is a special past-tense stem.

phases.pali: Katame cattāro?

tips: Used when asking to pick from a specific group
The -e ending marks a plural subject
The number four in its masculine plural form

phases.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;

tips: "Body in body" = seeing body AS body
kāya: The physical body or process
The -e ending means 'in' or 'regarding' the body (locative).
Identity shift: you become an observer
The -ī suffix means 'one who does this' — an observer.
vi-: Prefix meaning apart or intensive
The -ti ending marks the 3rd person singular — 'he dwells'.
The root 'bhikkh' relates to religious mendicancy
The -e ending is vocative plural — used when calling out to a group of monks.
The -ya ending makes this a gerund — 'having removed'.
